stories.headline: TRUMP EXTENDS IRAN CEASEFIRE AS MARKETS PRICE PERSISTENT UNCERTAINTY
stories.body: The U.S. extended its fragile two-week ceasefire with Iran, but investors remained cautious on whether de-escalation will actually reduce geopolitical risk premium. Oil prices surged 1.3% as traders continued to demand elevated risk premiums in crude markets despite the extension.

stories.headline: OIL RALLIES ON GEOPOLITICAL RISK, INFLATION FEARS RESURFACE
stories.body: WTI crude gained 1.3% to $90.86 and Brent rose 1.4% to $99.81 as persistent Middle East tensions keep crude elevated. The sustained $90+ floor is approximately 3x higher than 2021 levels, putting cumulative upside pressure on headline CPI expectations heading into next week's data release.

stories.headline: EQUITIES PAUSE RECOVERY AS INFLATION CONCERNS RESURFACE
stories.body: The S&P 500 edged down 0.11% to 7,082 as mixed signals dominated trading. Rising oil prices sparked inflation worries despite the positive ceasefire news. The sell-first-ask-questions-later dynamic typically seen in peak geopolitical uncertainty kept equity risk on the sidelines, though losses remained modest.

stories.headline: TREASURY YIELDS EDGE HIGHER ON OIL-DRIVEN INFLATION EXPECTATIONS
stories.body: The 10Y Treasury rose 2 bps to 4.30%, reflecting updated inflation expectations amid elevated crude prices and geopolitical risk. The yield curve tilt persists as oil shocks propagate through supply chains. Fed rate expectations for 2026 remain in flux pending next week's CPI print and Powell's Friday Economic Club speech.

stories.headline: MICHIGAN CLAIMS NATIONAL CHAMPIONSHIP, DUSTY MAY WINS FIRST TITLE AT HELM
stories.body: Michigan captured the NCAA Men's Basketball Championship on April 6, defeating Arizona at Lucas Oil Stadium in Indianapolis. The Wolverines' tournament run marks the program's second national title and Dusty May's first championship as head coach, securing a competitive tournament victory.

stories.headline: LAKERS EXTEND PLAYOFF LEAD OVER ROCKETS WITH LEBRON DOMINANT
stories.body: LeBron James compiled 28 points, 8 rebounds and 7 assists to lead Los Angeles to a 101-94 Game 2 victory, taking a 2-0 series lead over Houston. Marcus Smart added 25 points and elite defense. The first-round matchup features strong individual performances shaping playoff momentum.

stories.headline: 2026 NFL DRAFT SET TO BEGIN THURSDAY IN PITTSBURGH AMID TOP PROSPECT INTRIGUE
stories.body: The 2026 NFL Draft kicks off April 23-25 in Pittsburgh, featuring Ohio State (5 players), Alabama (2), and Miami (2) with multiple first-round prospects. 16 confirmed attendees headline the three-day event. Teams finalize evaluations as trades and negotiations intensify heading into Round 1 selections.

stories.headline: SPRING CAMPS CONCLUDE ACROSS FBS WITH MARYLAND SHOWCASE SCHEDULED FOR SATURDAY
stories.body: Spring football camps wrap up nationwide with Maryland hosting a Spring Showcase on April 25. New OC Clint Trickett has developed QB Malik Washington during spring drills, while highly-touted recruit Zion Elee brings elite pass-rush potential. Virginia Tech and other programs concluded camp evaluations.

sports.detail: Defense impressed in recent scrimmage vs. offense. Malik Washington excelling at QB. Spring game TBD.
Steele Venters (Gonzaga) commits to UW. Zoom Diallo enters transfer portal. Roster rebuilding underway.
2026 NFL Draft begins April 23 in Pittsburgh. Seahawks preparing selections across three days. Evaluations finalized.
Lost Game 1 to Magic, Cade Cunningham 39 pts. Down 0-1 in playoff series. Game 2 at 7 PM ET tonight.
Home vs. Athletics at 4:10 PM ET (1:10 PM PT). Logan Gilbert pitching. T-Mobile Park.

watchlist: CPI print early next week — March inflation data critical amid elevated oil prices
Fed Chair Powell speaks Friday at Economic Club of NY — rate expectations in flux
2026 NFL Draft begins April 23 in Pittsburgh — three-day event starts tomorrow
Pistons vs. Magic Game 2 tonight 7 PM ET — playoff intensity escalating
Mariners home game tonight 4:10 PM ET vs. Athletics — AL West standings

deepDive.title: Oil to CPI: How Geopolitical Shocks Become Inflation
content: Yesterday's Iranian ceasefire extension appeared as positive geopolitical development, yet WTI surged 1.3% to $90.86 while equity markets registered losses of 0.6%. The paradox deserves explanation: how does good geopolitical news produce negative market reactions? The answer lies in understanding how crude price movements transmit through inflation expectations to change the calculus for monetary policy and equity valuations. This chain of transmission is what makes oil shocks uniquely powerful across multi-asset portfolios and how traders price tail risk.

Crude prices influence inflation through two distinct channels. The first is direct: a 10% jump in oil eventually feeds 0.2–0.4% into headline CPI through gasoline at the pump and diesel derivatives. The second is indirect: oil's cost structure bleeds through supply chains—shipping, jet fuel, chemical feedstocks—hitting core inflation with a 3–6 month lag. This April's $90 oil floor is roughly 3x higher than 2021 trough lows, so every dollar of incremental volatility compounds meaningful CPI tail risk that cannot be ignored by portfolio managers.

Markets priced the ceasefire volatility in multiple layers. Investors initially cheered the uncertainty resolution, but 72-hour reflection surfaced a harder question: does a temporary 2-week ceasefire extension with explicit resumption language actually reduce oil's geopolitical risk premium, or merely delay eventual reckoning? Answer: neither. Oil remains sticky when geopolitical risk persists—traders demand premium at every spot price. Bond markets reflected this wisdom: the 10Y ticked 2 bps higher to 4.30% as inflation expectations re-anchored upward. Equities, facing simultaneous pressure from both risk-off sentiment and rising discount rates, opted for caution.

Here's the critical forward path: Watch next week's CPI print and Powell's Economic Club speech closely. If headline CPI surprises hot on the back of oil-driven energy costs, expect another 5–10 bps upward leg in yields and renewed equity pressure. That's the transmission mechanism fully articulated: oil → CPI → policy rate expectations → equity volatility. The ceasefire extended the timeline but didn't defuse the fundamental chain linking geopolitical shocks to monetary policy expectations to multi-asset markets.
